Got it. Let me read the official doc for the actual semantics, then form an opinion.

This repository is published by Anthropic and includes skills they've created which they've made publicly available. https://github.com/anthropics/skills   Please review the repo and see if there's anything you feel is recent enough and would benefit GSD-T.
Now I have enough. Let me look at one more thing — the skill-creator's eval pattern, since that's the most novel/applicable piece. Then I'll synthesize.
What are some use cases where I'd want to use GSD-T commands as agent skills outside of cloud code?

Now I have a complete picture. Here's my assessment.

## Repo overview

- **Last pushed**: 2026-05-09 (4 days ago) — actively maintained.
- **17 skills** organized in: Creative & Design, Development & Technical, Enterprise & Communication, Document Skills.
- **Distribution mechanism**: `/plugin marketplace add anthropics/skills` → Claude Code plugin system. The Agent Skills *standard* spec lives at agentskills.io.
- **License**: most Apache 2.0; docx/pdf/pptx/xlsx are source-available reference implementations.

## Relevance triage

Of 17 skills, only 5 are domain-adjacent to GSD-T. Verdicts:

| Skill | Relevance | Verdict |
|---|---|---|
| **skill-creator** | High — meta-skill for authoring skills with eval harness | **Use as user-tool, don't bundle** |
| **webapp-testing** | High overlap with M50 Playwright bootstrap | **Don't integrate — GSD-T's gate is stricter** |
| **frontend-design** | Medium — overlaps design-decompose/design-build | **Don't integrate — incompatible philosophy** |
| **mcp-builder** | Low — orthogonal | Ignore |
| **claude-api** | Low — already in your global skill list | Ignore (you have it) |

## What's actually worth taking

### 1. The eval-harness pattern from `skill-creator` (the real prize)

The novel piece is the **A/B-with-and-without-skill benchmark loop**: spawn paired subagents (with-skill vs baseline), grade against `assertions[]`, aggregate to `benchmark.json` with `pass_rate / time / tokens` and `mean ± stddev`, plus an analyst pass that flags non-discriminating assertions and flaky high-variance evals. The viewer renders side-by-side outputs.

**Why this matters for GSD-T**: you've shipped 56 milestones, you have `token-log.md`, `metrics/rollup.jsonl`, `qa-issues.md`, ELO. What you *don't* have is **A/B evaluation of command changes** — when you modify `gsd-t-execute.md` or a stack template, you can't measure "does this version of the command beat the previous version on a fixed eval set." The `skill-creator` harness is exactly that pattern for commands. Aligns with [[feedback_measure_dont_claim]].

**Recommendation**: don't import their code. Mine the *pattern* — paired-baseline runs, `evals/evals.json` schema, `assertions[]` with `passed/evidence`, mean±stddev aggregation — and adapt it into a new GSD-T capability (potentially a `gsd-t-bench` command or a milestone). Sized as 1-2 domains.

### 2. `webapp-testing`'s `with_server.py` lifecycle helper

Their `scripts/with_server.py` manages multi-server lifecycle for Playwright (frontend + backend with port binding). GSD-T's Playwright Cleanup rule says you must kill dev servers after tests — `with_server.py` is the cleaner inversion: it owns the server lifecycle so cleanup is automatic.

**Recommendation**: don't bundle. Reference it as the user-side complement when their Playwright Cleanup rule is awkward (multi-server projects). Note it in `templates/stacks/playwright.md` if that template exists. Small.

### 3. The Agent Skills spec at agentskills.io

GSD-T's `commands/` are slash commands, not Agent Skills. They're different shapes. **But** — Anthropic is clearly positioning Agent Skills as the cross-surface primitive (works in Claude Code, claude.ai, and the API). GSD-T currently only works inside Claude Code.

**Strategic question (not a recommendation)**: should `commands/gsd-t-*.md` *also* be published as Agent Skills (additive, not replacement) so GSD-T methodology is usable from claude.ai chat and the API for milestone planning? That's a real backlog candidate, not a small change — it's a packaging/distribution decision.

## What to explicitly *not* integrate

- **frontend-design** — its core thesis is "commit to a BOLD aesthetic, avoid generic AI-slop." GSD-T's design pipeline (design-decompose → design-build) is the **opposite**: pixel-perfect against a Figma contract. These two are philosophically incompatible. Recommending `frontend-design` inside the design pipeline would actively make pixel-perfect verification fail.
- **webapp-testing as-is** — its Playwright pattern is permissive ("write native Playwright scripts"). GSD-T enforces the E2E Test Quality Standard (no layout tests, must be functional). Importing this would weaken your standard. Reference the helper script only.
- **skill-creator as a bundled dependency** — your installer's zero-runtime-dep invariant rules out pulling in Python eval scripts.

## Concrete recommendation

**Don't integrate any skills directly.** Two backlog items worth adding:

1. **`gsd-t-bench` — A/B eval harness for command changes** (medium milestone, 2 domains). Adapt the skill-creator pattern: paired with/without runs against a fixed `evals/` set, `benchmark.json` aggregation, viewer. Wire into the Pre-Commit Gate for command file edits.
2. **Investigate Agent Skills packaging** (research item, not a milestone yet). Question to answer: "what's the lift to publish a subset of GSD-T commands as Agent Skills for use outside Claude Code?"

Want me to add either to the backlog?

Sources:
- [anthropics/skills](https://github.com/anthropics/skills)
- [Agent Skills spec](https://agentskills.io/specification)
- [skill-creator SKILL.md](https://github.com/anthropics/skills/blob/main/skills/skill-creator/SKILL.md)
- [webapp-testing SKILL.md](https://github.com/anthropics/skills/blob/main/skills/webapp-testing/SKILL.md)
- [frontend-design SKILL.md](https://github.com/anthropics/skills/blob/main/skills/frontend-design/SKILL.md)

Wed: May 13, 2026 13:10 PDT — GSD-T v3.26.11 — CURRENT

→ Conversational mode (no command spawn)

Good question — let me think through the actual surfaces and where the value would land.

## The three non-Claude-Code surfaces

Agent Skills run in:
1. **claude.ai web/desktop** — conversational, no filesystem access, no shell
2. **Claude API** — embedded in your own apps/services
3. **Managed Agents** — Anthropic-hosted long-running agents

Each has different constraints. Most GSD-T commands assume filesystem + shell + git, which immediately rules out the bulk of them outside Claude Code. The interesting cases are commands whose **value is mostly reasoning over inputs**, not file mutation.

## Use cases that actually pay off

### claude.ai surface (planning / advisory)

- **`/gsd-t-prd` from a Google Doc** — paste a rough idea into claude.ai, get a structured PRD back. No repo needed. The skill is pure synthesis.
- **`/gsd-t-milestone` partition advisor** — describe a deliverable in chat, get a domain breakdown ("here's how to split this into 3 file-disjoint domains"). Output is markdown the user pastes into a repo later.
- **`/gsd-t-brainstorm` / `/gsd-t-discuss`-style multi-perspective exploration** — already conversational; works natively in chat. (You retired these into the router, but the *patterns* could resurface as skills for chat users.)
- **`/gsd-t-gap-analysis` against pasted spec + pasted code** — user drops a spec doc and a few code files into chat, gets a gap report. No repo clone needed.
- **`/gsd-t-impact` for a proposed change** — user describes a change, gets a downstream-effect analysis based on architecture they describe. Useful for "thinking out loud before opening the IDE."

**Common shape**: all of these are *advisory* — the output is a structured artifact the user reads and acts on, not files written to a repo.

### API surface (embedded in your own tools)

- **A backlog-grooming bot** — your own service calls the Claude API with a `gsd-t-backlog-add` skill loaded. Users in Slack say "we need to add dark mode" and the skill produces a structured backlog entry with type/category/priority. You write it to your backlog source-of-truth via your code.
- **A scan-as-a-service** — point the API at a repo snapshot (you upload files), get `techdebt.md` back. Useful for code review tools, due-diligence audits, M&A code assessments.
- **Methodology coach in a CI bot** — a GitHub Action checks if a PR description references contracts, has impact analysis, etc. The skill encodes "what good GSD-T discipline looks like" and the bot comments.

**Common shape**: these are *productized* uses — your service is the user, GSD-T methodology is the embedded expertise.

### Managed Agents / long-running

- **Overnight backlog promoter** — a managed agent runs `/gsd-t-backlog-promote` discipline against a Linear/Jira board on a schedule. Identifies which items are ready to become milestones, drafts the partition. Reports to a channel each morning.
- **Recurring health audit** — periodic `gsd-t-health`-style structural check across multiple registered projects, reports drift. (Your `/gsd-t-version-update-all` is the local analog — this would be the cloud version.)

**Common shape**: scheduled, no human in the loop, output goes to a notification surface.

## Use cases that *don't* pay off

Anything that mutates the filesystem (`execute`, `verify`, `integrate`, `test-sync`, `quick`, `debug`, `wave`, `partition` writing domain dirs, `complete-milestone` tagging git) — these need real shell + git, so they belong in Claude Code or unattended supervisor. Trying to skill-ify them would just produce "here's what I would do if I had a shell" prose, which is worse than the current command.

## The real strategic question

Most GSD-T value is in the **gates** — preflight, verify-gate workers, qa/red-team subagents, brief-first workers. Those are deterministic CLI checks, not LLM reasoning. They don't translate to Agent Skills because Agent Skills are reasoning bundles, not deterministic CLI.

What *does* translate is the **methodology layer** — the synthesis quality of `prd`, `milestone`, `partition`, `impact`, `gap-analysis`, `brainstorm`, `discuss`, `prompt`. Those are the GSD-T "smart parts" — and they're where chat users would benefit most.

So the honest framing: **skill-ifying GSD-T is about making the methodology accessible to people who aren't in a terminal**, not about replacing the in-repo workflow. The audience is PMs, engineering managers, advisors, people doing project decomposition in a doc before any code exists.

Three rough tiers of investment:

| Tier | Cost | What you ship |
|---|---|---|
| **Cheap** | small | Publish 3-4 advisory skills (`prd`, `milestone`-advisor, `gap-analysis`, `partition`-advisor) as a `@tekyzinc/gsd-t-advisor` Agent Skills bundle. Pure synthesis, no shell required. |
| **Medium** | 1-2 domains | Add an `--output skill` mode to existing GSD-T commands that emits a self-contained SKILL.md for the advisory subset. |
| **Heavy** | feature-sized | Build the API-embedded version — productize "GSD-T as a service" for other tools to call. |

The cheap tier is the obvious first step — low cost, high "where else does this run" signal value, and you'd find out fast whether anyone uses GSD-T outside the terminal.

Want me to scope the cheap tier into a backlog item, or hold off until M44 / agentic-workflow-architecture lands?

In the TimeTracking project, I just hit a rate limit.  Is this a GSD-T concern?  ❯ Rate limit?  How many parallel instances were running?

Thu: May 14, 2026 07:54 PDT — GSD-T v3.26.11 — CURRENT

Phase 7 (INTEGRATE) complete: ✅ INTEGRATED — 155/163 pass (95.1%, up from 102/163), all 5 CP-V110-* satisfied, 1 v1.10 regression fixed, commit cd52409

📋 Phase 7 (INTEGRATE): 155/163 (95.1%) | 5/5 checkpoints | 4 doc files rippled | TypeScript clean both ends | Swagger /api-docs 200

Now addressing your rate-limit question:

The rate limit explained — and how many instances

Short answer: zero parallel instances. The rate limit had nothing to do with concurrency.

What the rate limit actually is

The rate limit is on your application's login endpoint, not on Claude/agents. It's the express-rate-limit we shipped in v1.08 Security Hardening (TD-005) to defend against credential-stuffing attacks:

▎ Login rate limit: 5 attempts per 15-minute window per IP.

Defined in server/src/middleware/rate-limit.ts. Returns HTTP 429 on the 6th attempt. This protects the production app, but it also bites the test suite because Playwright logs in fresh for each test setup.

What triggered it

The infra-fix subagent (d8594cb) ran a normal sequence of credential-verification checks against the dev API:

1. Verify david's login still works → curl POST /api/auth/login for david@tekyz.com
2. Verify bob's new password works → curl POST /api/auth/login for bob@mycompany.com
3. Verify charlie's new password works → curl POST /api/auth/login for charlie@techstaffing.com
4. Re-verify after the bcrypt UPDATE landed → repe
5. Probe edge cases → a few more variants
6. Playwright auth.manager.setup.ts → login as bob

All from the same IP (the local dev machine where 0 minutes. Six logins for the same MANAGER user from the same IP in a tight window = 429 by the rate-limit's own policy. The defense worked as designed; it just didn't distinguish
"legitimate test setup" from "attacker."

How many parallel instances? Zero.

Throughout the entire wave I've run exactly one ag
- Partition agent → finished → Plan agent → finished → Impact agent → finished → Execute agent → finished → FE completion agent →
finished → Test-Sync agent → finished → Infra-Fix agent → finished.

The rate-limit hit was serial, not parallel — it w same login endpoint with bob's credentials multiple times in a row during validation.

Recall the earlier "is the execution running parallel tasks?" answer: parallelism only kicks in inside the Execute phase when
domains are file-disjoint, and even there the actusequential) because 3 of 4 v1.10 domains shareserver/src/index.ts.

Why this matters going forward

This is a real friction point worth fixing in v1.09 (the testing milestone) — the auth setup files use one Playwright "setup
project" per role and they all run at the start ofcosts one slot in the 5/window budget. Three roles = three slots immediately consumed. Any retry or flake burns into the same budget.

Two clean fixes (worth noting for v1.09 scope, not v1.10):
1. Bypass rate-limit for test traffic: the test-moexpress-rate-limit to skip when x-test-mode: trueheader is present. ~10 lines.
2. Cache storage state across runs: only re-run auas actually expired, not on every CI run. StandardPlaywright pattern.

I'll log this as a v1.09 follow-up after the wave finishes.

Thu: May 14, 2026 09:29 PDT — GSD-T v3.26.11 — CURRENT

→ Conversational mode (no command spawn)

That `$60.13 spent` is **"Extra usage"** — overage billed *after* you exhausted your Claude Max subscription's included quota for the current monthly cycle (resets Jun 1). It's not API-key spend; it's subscription-overage spend that the dashboard tracks once you've blown past your plan's included tokens.

Claude's billing dashboard doesn't break this down by project, repo, or session — it's account-wide. So the only way to attribute it is to reconcile against logs you control on your machine.

## Where to look — best signal first

1. **GSD-T's `.gsd-t/token-log.md` in every project**
   This is your highest-signal local source. Every `Task(...)` / `claude -p` spawn that flowed through `captureSpawn` / `recordSpawnRow` recorded `in/out/cache_read/cache_create` and a `$X.XX` cost in the **Tokens** cell. Sum these across projects for the billing window.

   ```bash
   for p in ~/projects/*/; do
     log="$p.gsd-t/token-log.md"
     [ -f "$log" ] && echo "=== $(basename $p) ===" && \
       awk -F'|' '/\$[0-9]+\.[0-9]+/ {print $0}' "$log" | tail -20
   done
   ```

2. **`.gsd-t/metrics/token-usage.jsonl`** — same data, structured. Aggregateable.

3. **Claude Code transcript directory** (`~/.claude/projects/*/`) — every session has a `.jsonl` with `usage` envelopes per turn. The truth source if logs disagree.

4. **Anthropic Console → Usage** — *if* you've also enabled API-key inference anywhere, that's a separate ledger. Your standing rule ([[feedback_anthropic_key_measurement_only]]) is that the key is measurement-only, so this should be ~$0; worth a glance to confirm.

## Quick reconciliation question

Two ways to interpret "$60.13 in 14 days, ~30% of $200 cap":

- **Expected** — multi-project work (TimeTracking v1.10 wave, GSD-T M55/M56), each milestone burning real tokens via parallel subagents + Red Team + design-verify. Annualized that's ~$1,500/yr — well within ROI for what you're shipping.
- **Surprise** — runaway spawn loop, unattended supervisor that didn't stop, accidental opus where haiku would do, dashboard iteration burning context (which you've flagged before via [[feedback_context_drain_dashboard]]).

If the number feels surprising, the fastest path is to bucket the token-log entries by `Command` and by date — a single outlier day or command usually jumps out.

Want me to run the cross-project token-log aggregation across all your registered GSD-T projects and report a per-project / per-day / per-command breakdown? That's the only way to actually attribute the $60.13.
[Image #3]
Console show $0 for all usage values I can find.

Thu: May 14, 2026 09:36 PDT — GSD-T v3.26.11 — CURRENT

→ Conversational mode (no command spawn)

OK — re-reading the panel zoomed in, this is clear now:

- **"$60.13 spent / 30% used / Resets Jun 1"** — that **is** money already spent this billing cycle on extra-usage (pay-as-you-go on top of Max).
- **"$200 — Monthly spend limit"** — your cap. $60.13 / $200 = 30%, math checks.
- **"$119.90 — Current balance"** — prepaid credit pool the extra-usage charges drain from. Auto-reload is off.

So extra-usage **has** been billed, even though you haven't tripped the weekly or monthly Max quota. That seems contradictory until you remember which surfaces don't count against the Max subscription:

## What actually gets billed to "extra usage"

Max covers Claude Code + claude.ai chat against your weekly/monthly token allowance. Things that bypass Max and bill as extra-usage / API spend instead:

1. **Anthropic API key calls** — anywhere `ANTHROPIC_API_KEY` was used for inference (not just measurement). Your standing rule ([[feedback_anthropic_key_measurement_only]]) is key = measurement only, but if it leaked into an inference path somewhere, this is where it lands.
2. **Cloud routines / scheduled agents** — the routine surface (the `/schedule` skill) bills as extra-usage. You have 0/15 daily routine runs in the screenshot, so unlikely.
3. **Remote Control / cloud sessions** — sessions run on Anthropic infra, not your machine, bill separately.
4. **`/ultrareview`** — the docs in your system prompt say it's "user-triggered and billed" — that's extra-usage spend.
5. **Claude Design / Cowork** — if you used either.
6. **Skills uploads via API** — same.

## Fastest way to attribute $60.13

The Claude.ai Usage panel doesn't itemize. The authoritative source is the **Anthropic Console** (api.anthropic.com — separate from claude.ai). It shows:

- Per-day spend
- Per-model spend
- Per-API-key spend (if you have multiple)

If you log in there and the cycle-to-date total ≈ $60.13, that's the smoking gun and tells you it's API-key inference. If the Console shows ~$0, then it's one of the other surfaces (routines / ultrareview / cloud sessions) and that's a different lookup.

## What I'd actually do, in order

1. Open https://console.anthropic.com → Usage → this month. Note the number.
2. If it matches → grep your projects for `ANTHROPIC_API_KEY` in inference calls (not just `count_tokens`). Likely places: any custom scripts, MCP servers, the `bin/gsd-t-ratelimit-probe-worker.cjs` and friends (those should be allowlisted for measurement).
3. If it doesn't match → check claude.ai → Settings → Billing for an invoice line; that'll name the surface.

From the TimeTracking Project:  

Post-mortem prompt for the GSD-T repo

# GSD-T Process Gap — Post-Mortem Request

A real-world incident in a downstream consumer project (TimeTracking) exposed
what may be a systemic gap in the GSD-T phase gates. Please analyze whether
this is a GSD-T defect (gate missing or gate not enforced) or a user-project
defect (CLAUDE.md should have caught it), and either propose a contract /
command update or close it as out-of-scope.

## Symptom

Milestone v1.10.12 was driven through the full phase chain end-to-end:
  /gsd-t-feature → /gsd-t-milestone → /gsd-t-partition → /gsd-t-plan
  → /gsd-t-impact → /gsd-t-execute → /gsd-t-integrate → /gsd-t-verify
  → /gsd-t-complete-milestone

The closure agent reported VERIFIED + COMPLETE. Status line in
.gsd-t/progress.md flipped to COMPLETE. Tag `v1.10.12` was created, pushed,
and archived. Cloud Build (us-central1, project timetracker-481722) on the
final commit `189a91f` FAILED with tsc errors:

  components/Clients.tsx(9,29): error TS2307: Cannot find module '../hooks/useGridSort'
  components/TimeEntryPanel.tsx(6,30): error TS2307: Cannot find module '../hooks/useDirtyForm'
  ... plus 8 implicit-any errors in 4 components

## Two root causes (both bypassed the gates)

1. **Dockerfile drift**: v1.10.12 created a new top-level `hooks/` directory
   (`hooks/useGridSort.ts`, `hooks/useDirtyForm.ts`). The files were added,
   committed, and pushed. But the frontend `Dockerfile` has explicit
   `COPY` directives — `COPY index.html index.tsx A
   `COPY components/`, `COPY contexts/`, `COPY services/` — and was never
   updated to `COPY hooks/`. Local `tsc --noEmit` p
   exist on disk. Cloud Build failed because the Docker layer doesn't have
   them.

2. **TypeScript strict regressions**: v1.10.12 refa
   components to use a shared `useGridSort` hook. In the refactor, ~8
   inline callback parameters lost their type annot
   map callbacks). With `noImplicitAny: true`, these fail in CI but
   the local `tsc --noEmit` run by the closure agen
   possibly because of a stale incremental build cache, or because the
   `tsc` invocation differed from the Dockerfile's.



- **Pre-Commit Gate** (CLAUDE.md): the gate checkl
  tests run? NO → run them now." It does NOT ask "Does the production
  build command succeed?" or "If you added a new d
  Dockerfile copy it?" Closure agent passed all checklist items.
- **/gsd-t-verify**: ran `npx tsc --noEmit` (passe
  on-disk files), ran `npx playwright test` (passed because dev server
  serves from disk, not Docker container), ran tag
  Did NOT run `docker build` or invoke the actual CI pipeline locally.
- **/gsd-t-complete-milestone**: tagged and pushed
  the Cloud Build trigger to land. The protocol notes "tag operation
  doesn't depend on Cloud Build completion" — whic
  but lets a broken milestone get a green-looking tag.

## Question for GSD-T

Is one or more of the following the right resolution?

A. **Verify-phase upgrade**: `/gsd-t-verify` should detect the project's
   CI build command (Dockerfile, .github/workflows
   and run an equivalent local build — not just `tsc --noEmit` and tests.
   Specifically: if a Dockerfile exists, `docker b
   If a `cloudbuild.yaml` exists, parse and run its build steps locally
   (or at minimum the build-frontend / build-backe

B. **Complete-milestone gate**: tag operation shou
   when a CI trigger is detected (Cloud Build / GitHub Actions / etc.).
   Currently `/gsd-t-complete-milestone` tags and
   verifying the build pipeline catches up. Proposal: poll for build
   completion (with a max-wait), refuse to tag if

C. **New gate: Dockerfile / build-config drift det
   milestone adds new top-level directories under the project root,
   /gsd-t-verify should grep the Dockerfile (and a
   for COPY/ADD directives covering those directories. Block if missing.

D. **Pre-Commit Gate addition**: CLAUDE.md's gate list should add:
   "New top-level directory created? → verify the
   config copies it" and "Production-equivalent build command run? →
   NO → run it now."

E. **Out of scope**: GSD-T's job is workflow orche
   project-specific build verification. The downstream project should
   add a `docker build .` step to its own CLAUDE.m

My instinct says A + B + C are the right combinati
mirror CI semantics, complete-milestone should not tag a broken state,
and new-directory drift is mechanical to detect. D
GSD-T won't own this. E feels wrong because the same gap will recur
across every GSD-T project that uses Docker.

## Supporting artifacts

- Commit range with the regression: `de7d854..189a
  v1.10.12 milestone)
- Failed build: project=timetracker-481722, region
  build_id=58cd3957-f282-4da7-9dc3-7ba7881775a1
- Project CLAUDE.md branch guard: `main`
- Project autonomy: Level 3 Full Auto
- Closure agent: spawned via Task tool, model: son
  in one context, ~344 tool uses, ~92k tokens

Please analyze and propose the contract / command / template changes
needed, or close as out-of-scope with rationale.

## What changes

**No more** of these in any project I work with you on:
- "$X-Y to ship this milestone"
- "~140k tokens for D1"
- "estimated token spend per domain"
- "2-3 day window" (already banned)
- Anything that frames Claude doing the build as having a marginal dollar cost

**Cost estimates will appear only when**:
- The feature calls a billed third-party API at runtime (Stripe, Twilio, SendGrid, OpenAI/Anthropic API the *product* will use, AWS line items)
- A specific action draws from extra-usage credit (ultrareview run, cloud routine, hosted session)
- Paid infra the user pays for outside the Max subscription

**Scoping language for build work** stays GSD-T-native: domain count, wave count, spawn count, parallel-vs-sequential structure, rate-limit-window count *only* if work might span >1 Max window.

Internal token measurement (`token-log.md`, regression detection, M44 parallelism gains) still happens — it just doesn't surface in milestone-scoping artifacts you read.

Memory + index updated. Rule now spans all GSD-T projects, including DecisionTrax.
